# BRAND.md Quality Guide

> Annotated reference showing what separates a strong BRAND.md from a generic one. Each section shows a **good excerpt** (from a real brand book), a **bad counter-example**, and the **quality differentiator** to hit.
>
> **IMPORTANT: Match the STRUCTURAL QUALITY, not the specific content.** The good excerpts below are from an existing brand (a canvas/notes app with glass surfaces and amethyst palette). Your output will have completely different content — different product, different visual language, different archetype. What should be identical is the DEPTH: narrative prose for stories, specific triggers for emotional journeys, CSS-level formulas for materials, named animations with physics values. The excerpts illustrate the quality bar, not the answer.

---

## The Origin Story

**Quality differentiator:** Narrative prose with emotional stakes and cultural context. NOT bullet points, NOT a PRD summary.

**Good:**
> Conquistador is Spanish for "conqueror." Conquer the blank page. Conquer the grid. Conquer the conventional boundaries of how thought gets organized.
>
> The name is a declaration: thinking doesn't happen in rows and columns. It happens spatially. Ideas cluster, overlap, drift, connect across distances. Conquistador gives that mess a canvas and gets out of the way.

**Bad:**
> Conquistador is a note-taking app built in 2024. We founded it because existing tools were too rigid. Our goal is to help people organize their thoughts better.

**Why the good version works:**
- Opens with the name's meaning — immediate cultural anchor
- Uses active verbs tied to the product's core action ("conquer the blank page")
- Third paragraph explains the product philosophy, not just the feature set
- The reader feels the product's worldview, not just its function

---

## The Name

**Quality differentiator:** Etymology, cultural context, pronunciation, and 3-4 meanings tied to product/brand/experience. NOT "we named it X because it sounds good."

**Good:**
> **Primary reading:** "Con-kees-ta-dor." Spanish noun, from Latin "conquistare" (to conquer, to acquire).
>
> **Why a historical name, not a tech name:** A name rooted in exploration history signals that this tool has roots deeper than the latest productivity trend.
>
> **What "conquer" means for Conquistador:**
> - Conquer the blank page (infinite canvas, not empty documents)
> - Conquer the grid (spatial note placement, not rows)
> - Conquer isolation (real-time collaboration)
> - Conquer the paywall barrier (generous free tier)

**Bad:**
> We chose the name "Conquistador" because it sounds powerful and memorable. It reflects our mission to help users conquer their work.

**Why the good version works:**
- Pronunciation guide for non-obvious names
- Etymological depth (Latin root)
- Justifies why THIS type of name, not just why this name
- Each "meaning" maps to a specific product capability, not abstract aspirations

---

## Core Values

**Quality differentiator:** "X over Y" format where Y is a legitimate alternative. 2-3 sentences explaining what the value means in practice — specific behavioral standards.

**Good:**
> **Craft over features.** We ship fewer things, but every interaction feels considered. We will delay a release to get an animation curve right rather than ship a feature list. This means saying no to "power user" requests that would clutter the surface.

**Bad:**
> **Quality.** We believe in delivering the highest quality product to our users.

**Why the good version works:**
- "Craft over features" has a real tradeoff — features is a legitimate alternative
- Names a specific sacrifice ("delay a release to get an animation curve right")
- States what the value causes the team to say NO to
- The opposite value ("features over craft") is something real companies actually choose

---

## Personality Traits

**Quality differentiator:** Table with "What it means / What it doesn't mean" columns, product-specific examples in both columns.

**Good:**
> | **Refined, but not precious** | The UI uses glassmorphism and geometric typography because those choices serve clarity, not decoration. We never add visual flourish that slows someone down. | We don't treat the UI as a museum piece. If a glass effect hurts performance, it goes. |

**Bad:**
> | **Refined** | We are sophisticated and elegant | We are not sloppy |

**Why the good version works:**
- "But not precious" is the guardrail — prevents the trait from becoming its own extreme
- Both columns reference specific product decisions (glassmorphism, performance)
- A designer can make actual decisions from this row

---

## Emotional Journey Map

**Quality differentiator:** Touchpoint-by-touchpoint map (6-10 touchpoints) with specific emotions AND specific triggers. NOT just before/during/after.

**Good:**
> | **First encounter** | Intrigue | The name is unusual (Latin?). The geometric type stands out. The deep amethyst palette and glass surfaces aren't what you expect from a "notes app." |
> | **Hitting the free tier limit** | Respect | Clear message: you've reached 3 boards. Pro gives you unlimited. No guilt trip, no modal that blocks your work. |
> | **Telling someone** | Pride | "Try Conquistador. It's this canvas app with glass surfaces where you just... put sticky notes anywhere." |

**Bad:**
> | **Before** | Frustrated | Users are unhappy with current tools |
> | **During** | Happy | Users enjoy using the product |
> | **After** | Satisfied | Users feel good about their work |

**Why the good version works:**
- Each touchpoint is a specific moment in the product experience, not a vague phase
- Emotions are specific ("intrigue," "respect," "pride") not generic ("happy," "satisfied")
- Triggers name exact product elements, not abstract benefits
- The "hitting a limit" and "telling someone" touchpoints reveal brand character at friction points and advocacy moments — the most revealing brand tests

---

## Tone Range

**Quality differentiator:** 3 key contexts that define the range (marketing = expressive, product UI = neutral, errors = constrained). Each has a tone shift description AND a concrete example.

**Good:**
> Default dimensional position: Formal ←[X]→ Casual (30% casual), Serious ←---[X]→ Playful (40%), etc.
>
> | **Marketing / landing page** | Inviting + confident. Let the visuals speak. | "Break the page." |
> | **Product UI** | Minimal + warm. Functional but not cold. | "New note" not "Create a new note on this board." |
> | **Error / friction** | Calm + honest. What happened, what to do. | "Couldn't save that note. Retrying now. Your text is safe locally." |

**Bad:**
> | **Pricing page** | Professional | |
> | **Error messages** | Empathetic | |
> | **Social media** | Fun | |
> | [9 more rows with single-word descriptions and no examples...]

**Why the good version works:**
- 3 contexts define the full range — any surface can interpolate from these three
- Tone shift is described in two adjectives that CREATE tension (inviting + confident, calm + honest)
- Every row has a concrete copy example
- Doesn't overextend into copy production — 3 examples calibrate the voice, 9 examples IS copywriting

---

## Tagline

**Quality differentiator:** Primary tagline scored with V/F/U rubric. One tagline — platform variants belong in copywriting.

**Good:**
> **Primary:** "Break the page."
>
> V:3 F:2 U:3 = 8/9.
> - Visual: a page cracking open into infinite space — strong image
> - Falsifiable: most apps respect the page — this claims the opposite
> - Unique: tied to the infinite canvas concept — no competitor would say this

**Bad:**
> **Tagline:** "Think Better Together"

**Why the good version works:**
- Scored against a rubric (V/F/U) — prevents generic taglines from passing
- The competitor swap test: could Miro say "Think Better Together"? Yes. Could Miro say "Break the page"? No. That's the difference.
- One tagline in the brand system. Platform-specific variants (App Store subtitle, social bio) are produced by copywriting, which consumes the voice DNA from BRAND.md

---

## Product-Specific Brand Sections

**Quality differentiator:** Sections unique to THIS product that carry brand weight. Explains WHAT the element is AND WHY it reinforces positioning.

**Good:**
> ## Nature Theme as Brand Differentiator
>
> **1. Product differentiation:** No major canvas app ships an earth-tone theme with greenhouse glass surfaces.
> **2. Conversion driver:** Nature is a Pro feature ($5.99/month). The most visible upgrade.
> **3. Brand identity extension:** Connects the brand to the physical world: craft paper, olive ink, greenhouse glass.
> **4. Accessibility story:** Three themes means users choose the visual environment that works best.

**Bad:**
> ## Features
> We have a light mode and dark mode plus a special nature theme.

**Why the good version works:**
- A unique product feature is analyzed through 4 brand lenses (differentiation, conversion, identity, accessibility)
- Each point explains WHY this feature matters to the brand, not just WHAT it is
- This section couldn't exist in any other brand book — it's specific to this product

---

## Digital Touchpoints

**Quality differentiator:** Each touchpoint describes HOW the brand's visual, verbal, and interaction choices manifest at that specific surface.

**Good:**
> | **Landing page** | Full glass system. Layered translucent panels. Clash Display heroes. Floating glass-effect sticky notes. Three-theme preview. |
> | **Email** | Minimal. Clash Display for headlines (with Georgia fallback), General Sans for body (with system-ui fallback). |

**Bad:**
> | **Landing page** | Show product features |
> | **Email** | Send updates to users |

**Why the good version works:**
- Names specific design elements (glass system, Clash Display, three-theme preview)
- Even email gets typography specifications with fallback fonts
- A designer could build each touchpoint from this description alone

---

## Narrative Tension — Three Configurations Worked End-to-End

> **Reference, not template.** The three configurations below illustrate how `references/narrative-tension.md` plays out for **three different brand types**: founder-led indie SaaS, mid-size product-led company, and creator-corporate partnership. Each names the tension decisions in the change log, the brand-kit board choices that fall out of those decisions, and the failure mode the brand has to actively avoid.

### Configuration A — Founder-led indie SaaS

**Fictional brand:** `Burrow` — a single-founder tool for solo writers who want to plan, draft, and ship a long-form piece without a CMS. One developer, no funding, ~400 paying users.

**Tension 5 — Creator-led, team-collective, or institutional?** Creator-led. The founder writes every changelog, runs the newsletter, replies to support in their own voice. The brand is inseparable from the founder's name. If the brand voice attempts institutional polish, returning users immediately notice the impersonation.

**Tension 2 — Persona sustainability?** Founder posts roughly monthly to the newsletter and replies to most support tickets within a day. The brand promises *"a thoughtful tool, made by a writer, for writers"* — that's sustainable. The brand does NOT promise "we ship weekly in public" (founder doesn't have that cadence) or "we respond instantly" (founder takes evenings off). The Change Log line: `Persona: creator-led, founder-driven, monthly newsletter + day-of support — operator-confirmed.`

**Tension 1 — Curated promise vs lived reality?** Origin Story names the actual constraint: built nights and weekends while founder's day job pays the bills. Values include `Slow Software over Feature Velocity` — defensible because the public roadmap has stayed deliberately short. Nothing in BRAND.md claims more capacity than exists.

**Tension 3 — Progress through struggle?** The brief mentions burnout from a prior failed startup. Personality-agent uses this **lightly** — the brand voice is calm and craft-oriented (an honest reaction to the prior over-scoped failure), not "I rose from the ashes." The Change Log: `Struggle arc: applied at low intensity — prior over-scoped startup informed scope discipline. Avoiding hero-recovery framing.`

**Tension 4 — Comeback / return arc?** No. Burrow is a debut product, not a return. Omitted entirely. Change Log: `Comeback arc: not applicable — debut product.`

**Brand-kit board choice:** Visual mode = *Light Editorial / Compliance*-adjacent (warm ivory, deep blue accent, paper grain), logo method = M1 monogram + meaning (B-as-burrow-opening with a soft cut suggesting depth). 3×3 board, panel 4 = quiet tagline `"Write deeper, not faster."` Panel 7 (physical) = a thin paper notebook crop, not a glossy device render.

**Failure mode this brand actively avoids:** Importing influencer / "build-in-public" rhetoric the founder doesn't actually do. The voice is craft-thoughtful, not founder-flex.

---

### Configuration B — Mid-size product-led company

**Fictional brand:** `Steerline` — a B2B route-optimization platform for mid-market logistics teams. 80-person company, Series B, named CEO + named VP of Engineering + named Design lead all appear publicly. ~6 years old.

**Tension 5 — Creator-led, team-collective, or institutional?** Team-collective. Three named voices are public (CEO sets strategic direction, VP Eng writes technical posts, Design lead publishes case studies). Brand voice is consistent across the three without being identical. Going institutional would feel like a step away from the trust the team has built; going single-creator-led would erase the engineering and design contributions.

**Tension 2 — Persona sustainability?** Each named voice posts at a known cadence (CEO quarterly, VP Eng monthly, Design lead bi-monthly). Brand never promises a rhythm any one of them can't keep. The brand DOES promise enterprise SLA support (real team behind it) and product reliability metrics (verifiable in status-page history).

**Tension 1 — Curated promise vs lived reality?** The Origin Story names the actual founding constraint: built by ex-logistics engineers who got tired of the existing platforms' opaque pricing. Values include `Transparent Pricing over Enterprise Negotiation` — defensible because the pricing page lists tiers publicly, no "contact sales" wall. If the brand can't keep that pricing-transparency promise (e.g., decides to add a custom-quote tier), the value gets re-written, not silently broken.

**Tension 3 — Progress through struggle?** Not foregrounded. The brand has had a normal Series-A-to-B trajectory — no near-death moments worth telling. Personality-agent does NOT manufacture a "we almost died" arc. Change Log: `Struggle arc: not applicable — normal growth trajectory; brand earns trust through capability, not adversity.`

**Tension 4 — Comeback / return arc?** No.

**Brand-kit board choice:** Visual mode = *Dark Product / Operator* (black + amber + muted accent). Logo method = M5 construction geometry — a modular mark built from the same rule that produces icon variants. 3×3 board, panel 3 (digital application) = a thin slice of the actual product UI (route map fragment with one optimized line), panel 9 (system detail) = the construction primitives that produce the mark.

**Failure mode this brand actively avoids:** Pretending to be smaller than it is. "Made by a small team" rhetoric would lie — the team is 80 people. The brand's institutional polish has to be honest, not borrowed-creator-warmth.

---

### Configuration C — Creator-corporate partnership

**Fictional brand:** `Steady` by Calmwell — a sleep-aid supplement co-developed by a named sleep researcher (Dr. Mira Onyebuchi) and a wellness DTC company (Calmwell, 30-person team). The researcher's name is on the bottle; Calmwell handles manufacturing, fulfillment, regulatory, marketing infrastructure.

**Tension 5 — Creator-led, team-collective, or institutional?** Hybrid by design — and the brand book has to name which surfaces hold which voice. Dr. Onyebuchi's voice appears on the science page, the newsletter "From the lab" section, and the long-form podcast appearances. Calmwell's institutional voice appears on the FAQ, shipping policy, return policy, customer support, and the legal pages. Strategy-agent's Change Log: `Mode: creator-led on science + content surfaces (named voice — Dr. Onyebuchi), institutional on commerce + policy surfaces (Calmwell brand). Transition trigger: any surface that names a regulated claim or a transactional commitment shifts to the institutional voice.`

**Tension 2 — Persona sustainability?** Dr. Onyebuchi commits to a quarterly long-form essay + one podcast per month — that's it. Brand does NOT promise weekly content from her (her research demands the bulk of her time). Calmwell's customer support is staffed 5 days/week — brand promises business-day responses, not 24/7.

**Tension 1 — Curated promise vs lived reality?** The Origin Story carefully names what each partner contributes. Dr. Onyebuchi's role is named honestly: "co-developed and scientifically directed by," not "made by." Reverse honesty matters too — Calmwell's manufacturing role is named, not hidden behind the researcher's name. The product is not styled as "a single researcher in a lab" when it's a partnership.

**Tension 3 — Progress through struggle?** Not foregrounded. The brand earns trust through Dr. Onyebuchi's credentials and Calmwell's track record — not through an adversity arc. The temptation would be to import Dr. Onyebuchi's personal academic-career struggles into the brand voice; personality-agent declines. Change Log: `Struggle arc: not applicable — credentials and track record carry trust, not adversity rhetoric.`

**Tension 4 — Comeback / return arc?** No, with one nuance: Calmwell has prior products in market, so first-time visitors to the Steady brand may already know Calmwell. The Emotional Journey "first encounter" briefly acknowledges this for returning Calmwell customers (different framing on the landing-page hero), but the Steady brand itself is not framed as a "return."

**Tension 4 critical check — exploitation framing?** The strongest temptation: styling sleep deprivation, anxiety, or insomnia as something the brand "understands deeply" because Dr. Onyebuchi has lived with it. Personality-agent FAILs this temptation. The brand earns trust through what the *product does differently* (named clinical mechanisms, dosing transparency, published study citations), not through performing identification with the audience's pain. Change Log: `Pain-narrative gate: refused — brand earns trust through product mechanism, not lived experience.`

**Brand-kit board choice:** Visual mode = *Light Editorial / Compliance* (warm ivory, deep blue, controlled gold accent). Logo method = M3 metaphor fusion (researcher mark + steady wave). 3×3 board has TWO logo-related panels: panel 1 = Steady wordmark + mark; panel 9 = a small `by Calmwell` lockup at peripheral size — so the partnership is visible without subordinating the named researcher.

**Failure mode this brand actively avoids:** Two — (1) hiding the corporate partner to imply the product is a lone-researcher operation, and (2) styling the researcher's voice with marketing-DTC warmth she doesn't actually use.

---

### How to read these three

Each configuration is structurally identical (same five tension dimensions, evaluated against the same four critic questions) but produces three visually and verbally distinct BRAND.md outputs. **The framework is consistent; the answers are brand-specific.** When a brief comes in that doesn't fit one of these three configurations cleanly, the strategy-agent's Change Log states which dimensions are load-bearing and which are absent by design.

The single most common mistake across all three: **forcing a tension dimension that the brief doesn't actually support** (manufactured struggle, borrowed comeback, creator-led voice on an institutional brand). Critic-agent NT-Q1 through NT-Q4 catch these, but writing them in at strategy-agent / personality-agent saves the rewrite cycle.
